proof_trace: In processInputBuffer (L1573-1589), after processCommand(c) returns C_OK, the code accesses c->flags, c->read_reploff, c->querybuf, c->qb_pos (L1574-1576) and calls resetClient(c) (L1584) before checking if (server.current_client == NULL) break (L1589). The developer comment at L1586-1588 explicitly acknowledges: 'freeMemoryIfNeeded may flush slave output buffers. This may result into a slave, that may be the active client, to be freed.' The NULL guard is misplaced — it comes after the UAF-prone accesses, not before them. If processCommand(c) returns C_OK while c has been freed (via freeMemoryIfNeeded → flushSlavesOutputBuffers → writeToClient → freeClient(c)), all accesses to c between L1574 and L1584 are use-after-free.
attack_scenario: A replica client connected to a Redis master under maxmemory pressure sends REPLCONF ACK; processInputBuffer processes the command with server.current_client = c (the slave); freeMemoryIfNeeded triggers flushSlavesOutputBuffers which calls writeToClient on this same slave, a write failure causes freeClient(c) freeing the client struct; processCommand still returns C_OK, and the subsequent reads/writes to c->flags, c->reploff, and resetClient(c) all access freed heap memory, enabling potential heap corruption.

proof_trace: In `aeProcessEvents`, `fe = &eventLoop->events[fired[j].fd]` is pinned at L418 before invoking `fe->rfileProc()` at L443. A readable callback (`readQueryFromClient`) can process `CONFIG SET maxclients`, which calls `aeResizeSetSize` → `zrealloc(eventLoop->events, ...)`, freeing the old events array and updating `eventLoop->events` to the new allocation. After the callback returns, L448-459 read `fe->mask`, compare `fe->wfileProc`, and call `fe->wfileProc(...)` through the now-stale pointer into freed memory; the code comment at L437-441 acknowledges callbacks may modify events but only guards the mask-cleared case, not a realloc-induced invalidation of `fe`.
attack_scenario: An authenticated Redis client (default Redis has no requirepass) builds up a pending write buffer to activate AE_WRITABLE on its fd, then sends `CONFIG SET maxclients <large_value>` pipelined in the same request; when `epoll_wait` returns AE_READABLE|AE_WRITABLE for that fd in one fired entry (confirmed in ae_epoll.c:118-128), the CONFIG SET processing triggers `zrealloc(eventLoop->events)`, leaving `fe` dangling; subsequent `fe->wfileProc(eventLoop,fd,fe->clientData,mask)` at L450 dereferences stale memory, enabling potential control-flow hijack if the freed region is reclaimed with attacker-influenced data.

proof_trace: In the `fail:` path of `getRedisConfig`, line 284 calls `redisFree(c)` which frees the `redisContext` struct, but lines 287-290 immediately dereference fields of the freed context (`c->connection_type`, `c->tcp.host`, `c->tcp.port`, `c->unix_sock.path`) to print an error message. Every error branch (connection failure, CONFIG GET returns REDIS_REPLY_ERROR, non-array reply) reaches this UAF since `c` is always non-NULL at that point. The `freeClient` post-free use of `c` in `listSearchKey` (L315,318) is not a dereference — adlist uses pointer-value comparison only — so that pattern is benign.
attack_scenario: A Redis server configured with ACL restrictions (`aclsetuser default reset`) will reject `CONFIG GET` with a NOPERM error reply, triggering `goto fail` in `getRedisConfig`; `redisFree(c)` frees the context, and the subsequent `c->connection_type` read accesses freed heap memory, causing a crash or potential heap-data disclosure if an attacker controls the server response timing.

proof_trace: In `activeDefragSdsListAndDict` at L358, `dictGetHash(d, sdsele)` is called after `activeDefragSds(sdsele)` already freed the underlying allocation, making `sdsele` a dangling pointer; `dictGetHash` calls `d->type->hashFunction(sdsele)` which reads bytes of freed memory (confirmed UAF read). The correct pattern (used in `defragKey` L778) hashes the *new* pointer (`de->key` = `newsds`), not the old one; if the wrong hash is produced, `replaceSateliteDictKeyPtrAndOrDefragDictEntry` silently fails, leaving `repl_scriptcache_dict` with a stale freed key pointer that will be dereferenced on the next replication script-cache lookup.
attack_scenario: An attacker floods Redis with SCRIPT LOAD commands to populate `repl_scriptcache_fifo`/`repl_scriptcache_dict` and induce heap fragmentation sufficient to trigger `activeDefragCycle` → `defragOtherGlobals` → `activeDefragSdsListAndDict`; when a script key sds is defragged and the freed-memory hash diverges from the true hash, the dict retains the stale pointer, and the next replication script-cache check (e.g., `EVALSHA` on a replica) dereferences the freed/reused memory.

proof_trace: In clusterReadHandler, after clusterProcessPacket returns 1 (link assumed valid), lines 2195-2196 access link->rcvbuf and write to link. However, clusterProcessGossipSection (called at line 1999 inside clusterProcessPacket) can call freeClusterLink(node->link) when a gossip entry targets link->node — which is the same struct — if that node has PFAIL/FAIL set and the gossip reports a different IP/port. freeClusterLink frees both link->rcvbuf and link itself (zfree), but clusterProcessPacket still returns 1 because no return-value signaling exists from clusterProcessGossipSection, causing clusterReadHandler to read/write freed memory at lines 2195-2196.
attack_scenario: A malicious cluster node in PFAIL state (link still active during the timeout/2-to-timeout window, or after reconnect before PONG clears PFAIL) sends a crafted PING message whose gossip section includes the sender's own node ID with a different IP/port; this triggers freeClusterLink on the in-flight link inside clusterProcessGossipSection, after which clusterReadHandler performs a use-after-free and double-free on the released link and its rcvbuf.

proof_trace: `reconnectingRedisCommand` accepts `redisContext *c` by value and internally calls `redisFree(c)` + reassigns `c` during IO/EOF reconnection (line 1246), but the caller's `src_ctx` variable is never updated, leaving it as a dangling pointer. Lines 5986, 6025, and 6047 each pass the dangling `src_ctx` to `reconnectingRedisCommand` (triggering a read of freed memory at the `assert(!c->err)` on line 1239), and the cleanup `redisFree(src_ctx)` at line 6068 produces a double-free of the originally allocated context.
attack_scenario: An attacker hosting a malicious Redis server referenced via `--cluster-from malicious_ip:port` can accept the TCP connection, receive the `INFO` command, then close the socket (EOF); this triggers the reconnection branch inside `reconnectingRedisCommand`, freeing `src_ctx` while the caller retains the dangling pointer — all subsequent calls to `reconnectingRedisCommand(src_ctx, ...)` at lines 5986/6025/6047 read freed heap memory, and the cleanup path double-frees it.

proof_trace: In quicklistRotate, when the quicklist has exactly one node (len==1), ziplistGet sets value = p+entry.headersize — a pointer into node->zl. quicklistPushHead then calls __ziplistInsert, which invokes ziplistResize (zrealloc) on the same buffer at L790 of ziplist.c, potentially freeing and relocating it; the subsequent memcpy(p, s, slen) at L833 reads from s==value, which is now a dangling pointer. The code explicitly re-fetches p for the single-node case (L1307-1309) but fails to re-fetch or copy value before the realloc, leaving a use-after-free read on any non-integer-encoded string element. Reachability is uncertain: quicklistRotate has no callers in the server command handlers (t_list.c, server.c) in this codebase version, only appearing in internal test code.
attack_scenario: If quicklistRotate is exposed via a command or module, an attacker who pushes non-integer string elements into a Redis list small enough to fit in a single quicklist node and triggers rotation (e.g., via RPOPLPUSH to self or a future LROTATE command) can cause the server to read from freed ziplist memory, potentially corrupting the head element written into the list.
